---
title: "Research"
site: distill::distill_website
---
# Working Papers

## Identifying The Income Effect On Nutrition For Agricultural Households: Separability Of Production And Consumption

I investigate the characterization of the Indian agricultural market using a panel data of 960 households in Maharashtra state. I find that production and consumption are separable in the rural agriculture market. Using a multinomial logistic regression model, I find that income increase of households improve the nutrition measured as diet diversity score (ranging from 0 to 10) establishing the link between from production to consumption preferences. In fact, women of the households consume an additional food group if the agricultural income from sale of crops increase by rupees 500. On the other hand, regressing the diet diversity score on the health variable of the women show no significant result. This goes to establish that there is no dependence of labor characteristics on consumption preferences, that is production is independent of consumption preferences. 

## CO2 Fertilization: Assessing the Impact of Carbon Dioxide on Agricultural Yield

In this paper I validate the result that carbon dioxide improves crop yield under field conditions, which hitherto was only part of controlled field experiments and crop science studies. Using the nascent remote sensing atmospheric carbon dioxide from NASA’s Orbiting Carbon Observatory – 2 and the agricultural yield data obtained from ICRISAT-TCI I identify that elevated atmospheric carbon dioxide increases yield of both wheat and rice over the period 2014 – 17 in India. I make use of the solar induced fluorescence (SIF) to overcome simultaneity between carbon dioxide in the atmosphere and crop yields, since carbon dioxide is both absorbed and released by plants in the two different physiological processes of photosynthesis and respiration. An increase of one part per million carbon dioxide in the atmosphere raises rice yield measured in kilograms per hectare by 0.5 percent, and wheat yield increases by 1.3 percent.
